title: Te Whakapakari Tinana me te Wairua: Strengthening Body and Spirit through Ngāti Kahungunu Practices
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the traditions of Ngāti Kahungunu, specifically focusing on the healing properties and cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) that flows near our college. Students will explore how our tūpuna used the stream's resources for physical healing, spiritual cleansing, and community wellbeing, connecting these ancient practices to contemporary health concepts.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional healing practices connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and explain their health benefits
Compare and contrast traditional Māori health concepts (hauora, tapu, noa) with Western health models through practical application
Design and demonstrate a simple rongoā (traditional remedy) using local plants from our stream environment
Collaboratively create a pepeha that acknowledges personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its role in wellbeing

activities.title: Whakatau me te Pepeha: Connecting to Our Place
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students learn and share their pepeha, specifically including their relationship to the local waterway. Through guided discussion, explore how connection to place (tūrangawaewae) forms the foundation of Māori health concepts.

activities.title: Hīkoi Takutai: Streamside Learning Journey
activities.description: Guided visit to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua. Students identify healing plants (rongoā) traditionally used by Ngāti Kahungunu, learn proper harvesting protocols (rahui, tikanga), and understand the spiritual significance of water (wai) in cleansing rituals.

activities.title: Mahi Rongoā: Traditional Remedy Workshop
activities.description: Hands-on workshop creating simple rongoā such as kawakawa balm or harakeke cordial using traditional methods. Students document the process, identify active health components, and compare the holistic approach with Western medicine concepts.

activities.title: Whare Tapa Wha in Action: Holistic Health Analysis
activities.description: Using the Whare Tapa Wha model, students analyze how traditional practices from the stream address taha tinana (physical), taha wairua (spiritual), taha whānau (social), and taha hinengaro (mental) wellbeing. Create visual representations showing these connections.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga Challenge: Protecting Our Healing Resource
activities.description: Student groups develop and present practical action plans for protecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku as a continuing source of community health. Plans must incorporate both traditional knowledge and modern conservation science.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith (local knowledge holder in rongoā and stream traditions)
'Rongoā Māori: Traditional Māori Healing' by Rob McGowan (specific chapter on Ngāti Kahungunu practices)
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Plant Identification Guide (college-developed resource)
Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website rongoa resources section (https://www.kahungunu.iwi.nz/te-reo-me-ona-tikanga)

tasks: Create and present a digital story documenting one traditional health practice from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Develop a comparative chart analyzing how traditional Māori health concepts align with modern health science
Participate in a group demonstration of proper tikanga for harvesting and preparing rongoā
Complete a reflective journal connecting personal wellbeing to connection with local environment

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal Health and Physical Development
Health and Physical Education: C1 - Relationships with Other People
Health and Physical Education: D1 - Healthy Communities and Environments
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
